answers.intake_q1: When did the elbow pain start?
answers.intake_q2: Can you describe where exactly on your elbow the pain is located?
answers.intake_q3: What activities or injuries, if any, happened before the pain started?
answers.intake_a3: I was playing tennis when the pain started
answers.intake_q4: Any numbness, tingling, or weakness in your arm or hand?
answers.intake_q5: Any swelling, redness, or warmth around the elbow?
answers.intake_q6: Have you tried any treatments or medications for the elbow pain, and if so, what was the effect?
answers.intake_a6: I iced the elbow the first day and it help a little with the pain.
answers.intake_q7: Does the pain worsen with gripping or lifting objects, especially during activities like playing tennis?
answers.intake_a7: The pain is worse when I play tennis and when I lift objects
answers.intake_q8: Any prior history of elbow problems or other joint issues?
answers.intake_q9: Any fever, chills, or other systemic symptoms?
answers.intake_summary: A patient presents with 3 weeks of lateral elbow pain that started during tennis, worsens with gripping and lifting, without numbness, swelling, or systemic symptoms. Initial icing provided mild relief. No prior joint issues or systemic symptoms are reported.

note.soap:
S: 
The patient is a 45-year-old female presenting with a 3-week history of pain localized to the outside of her elbow. The pain began while playing tennis and worsens with gripping and lifting objects, particularly during tennis activities. She denies numbness, tingling, weakness, swelling, redness, warmth, fever, chills, or other systemic symptoms. Initial treatment with ice provided some mild relief. There is no prior history of elbow or joint problems. Patient is not pregnant.

O: 
Phone evaluation only; no physical exam or vital signs available.

A: 
The clinical presentation is consistent with lateral epicondylitis (tennis elbow), characterized by lateral elbow pain exacerbated by gripping and lifting activities, with onset related to tennis play. There are no signs of systemic illness or neurological involvement.

Differential: 
1. Lateral epicondylitis (tennis elbow) 
2. Radial tunnel syndrome 
3. Osteoarthritis of the elbow 
4. Referred pain from cervical radiculopathy 

P: 
- First-line medication: Nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drugs (NSAIDs) such as ibuprofen or naproxen for pain relief and inflammation reduction. 
- Alternatives: Acetaminophen if NSAIDs are contraindicated or not tolerated. 
- Medication safety considerations: Use NSAIDs with caution in patients with gastrointestinal, renal, or cardiovascular risk factors; ensure adequate hydration and avoid prolonged use without medical supervision. 
- Return precautions: Advise the patient to seek care if pain worsens, if there is new onset of numbness, tingling, weakness, swelling, redness, or warmth around the elbow, or if systemic symptoms such as fever develop. 
- Testing: Not indicated at this time given the typical presentation; consider imaging or referral if symptoms persist beyond 6-8 weeks or if diagnosis is uncertain.

evidence.title: Tennis elbow - NHS
evidence.snippet: rvices without needing a referral from a GP. Get NHS help for back or joint problems Treatments for tennis elbow Tennis elbow usually gets better after resting your arm for a few weeks, but it can sometimes last longer. A GP may give you anti-inflammatory tablets. They may also refer you for physiotherapy if your symptoms have not improved after trying treat…

evidence.title: content.txt
evidence.snippet: Check if you have tennis elbow The main symptom of tennis elbow is pain on the outside of your elbow. The pain may range from mild discomfort when you move your arm to constant pain that can affect your sleep. It may be worse when: - lifting or bending your arm - gripping objects - moving your wrist Other symptoms of tennis elbow include: - tenderness or swe…

evidence.title: Elbow Injuries | Elbow Disorders | MedlinePlus
evidence.snippet: bone, cartilage, ligaments and fluid. Muscles and tendons help the elbow joint move. When any of these structures is hurt or diseased, you have elbow problems. Many things can make your elbow hurt. A common cause is tendinitis , an inflammation or injury to the tendons that attach muscle to bone. Tendinitis of the elbow is a sports injury , often from playin…

evidence.title: Tendinitis: MedlinePlus
evidence.snippet: onnect muscles to bones. They help your muscles move your bones. Tendinitis is the severe swelling of a tendon. Tendinitis usually happens after repeated injury to an area such as the wrist or ankle. It causes pain and soreness around a joint. Some common forms of tendinitis are named after the sports that increase their risk. They include tennis elbow, golf…
